title: Configurator and polish: add wizard, prune command, docs
description:
The dbfresh add interactive wizard emitting YAML; the dbfresh prune retention command; docs via material for mkdocs.
Accept: the wizard appends a valid block that run executes; prune enforces retention. Spec: dbfresh.md section 13.
close_reason: Superseded by the E1-E11 epic plan in dbfresh.md

title: TUI robustness: worker execution and guarded error paths
description:
From the v1 review (df-x0e). The TUI is a second front-end over the same engine/store; today it is fragile at every non-happy path.

P1 -- action_run_checks calls run_and_persist synchronously in the event handler, so the UI freezes for the entire multi-source run. Use Textual's @work(thread=True) / run_worker.

P1 -- The run action crashes the whole app if any source's create_adapter fails (the connect-time crash reaches Textual unhandled); spec 11.3 wants graceful degradation.

P1 -- ConfigureScreen._propose constructs the adapter OUTSIDE its try/finally (line 80): an unreachable source crashes the screen instead of showing the error in #proposal-text.

P1 -- _on_configure_dismissed to _reload_config to load_config can raise after a successful write (the duplicate-check_id case), so the app crashes immediately after doing exactly what the user asked. Guard the reload.

P2 -- _accept writes to target_files(...)[0] -- the first included file, silently -- where the CLI wizard prompts for the target; divergent behavior between the two front-ends, plus a possible IndexError. Align with the CLI and guard the index.
close_reason: TUI robustness: the Run action runs run_and_persist in a Textual worker thread so the UI stays responsive and a cancelled/errored run leaves the dashboard untouched; Configure guards adapter construction (an unreachable source shows an error in the proposal area instead of crashing the screen) and guards the post-write config reload (keeps the last-good config and notifies); the Configure target-file is resolved once and surfaced when multiple included files match. 662 tests, no snapshot changes.

title: TUI first-run onboarding
description:
Umbrella for getting a brand-new user from zero to a working setup entirely inside the TUI -- today impossible at every step.

dbfresh ui refuses to start without an existing config (cli.py:537-541), unlike dbfresh add which tolerates a missing file (cli.py:421); an empty-but-valid config loads into a blank grid with no guidance (populate_grid always draws the header row); and with zero sources the Configure Select is empty so no source, and therefore no check, can be created (df-ymt).

Children: the new-source flow in Configure (df-ymt, backend already exists), tolerating/creating a missing config in ui, a Home empty-state message, and a post-Accept prompt connecting configuration to a first run.

title: Let dbfresh ui start without an existing config
description:
_ui_command exits with a config error when the file is missing (cli.py:537-541), unlike dbfresh add which tolerates absence and bootstraps (cli.py:421).

Let ui either start against an empty in-memory config or offer to create a minimal starter config.yaml, so a brand-new user can reach the Configure screen without first hand-writing YAML. Pairs with df-ymt (the new-source flow they would use next).

title: Show a Home empty-state message when no checks are configured
description:
An empty-but-valid config loads into a bare grid: populate_grid always draws the header row (dashboard.py:219-231) and app.py substitutes no empty-state.

When there are zero checks (or zero sources), replace the empty grid with guidance ("no checks configured -- press c to Configure").

title: Offer to run checks immediately after Accept
description: After Configure writes new checks, they render as unknown on Home until the next run, with nothing connecting the two steps. Add a post-Accept "run now?" affordance or hint so a first-time user sees results without hunting for the r key.

title: Documentation: mkdocs-material site with build-time reference generation
description: mkdocs-material site per spec; build-time just docs reference generation from the registries. See dbfresh.md epic E9.
close_reason: mkdocs-material site under docs/ with every section-14 page; registry.py + docsgen.py generate the metric/operator/matrix/CLI reference tables at build time from compile_metric_sql, parse_expectation, configurator.category_offers, and cli.build_parser; just docs regenerates and runs mkdocs build --strict (verified green); parity tests in tests/test_registry_parity.py and tests/test_docsgen.py fail on registry/code drift.

title: Harden the add wizard and configurator
description:
From the v1 review (df-x0e). Both authoring surfaces (the add wizard and the TUI Configure screen) share one module but have diverged and are the least robust code in the repo.

P1 -- dbfresh add forces real secrets into committed YAML: the new-source flow probes with the literal key=value params typed and writes them verbatim; typing token=${DATABRICKS_TOKEN} fails the probe (never interpolated), so the only way past the mandatory connection test is to type the real secret, which is then written into the config that by design must never hold secrets (spec 4, principle 3). Interpolate ${VAR} tokens before probing but write the uninterpolated form to YAML; nudge toward ${VAR} for credential-looking keys.

P1 -- Wizard robustness: the adapter opened in _select_source is closed only on the straight-line path; early returns (user declines) and every exception path leak the connection (no try/finally). int()/float() on prompts crash with tracebacks on non-numeric input; files[idx] can IndexError and a negative number indexes from the end. Running add twice for the same object appends duplicate check blocks whose derived check_ids collide, so the NEXT load_config rejects the config the wizard just wrote (every command then exits 2 until hand-edited) -- append_checks must re-validate or dedupe before writing. Dead config_path param; the wizard functions are unannotated while the rest of the codebase is fully annotated.

P2 -- Timestamp ambiguity is never prompted (spec 11.1: if several match, ask). pick_timestamp_column returns needs_choice plus candidates, but both add and Configure silently drop the freshness proposal; docs/authoring-checks.md claims they present the candidate list. Prompt from TimestampChoice.candidates; add a selection widget in Configure.

P2 -- Neither front-end passes is_view into propose_checks (cli.py, tui/configure.py), so a Databricks view with no timestamp candidate gets an invalid describe_history proposal. One-line fix each: is_view=existence.info.is_view.

P2 -- add/Configure rewrite whole config files via yaml.safe_dump, destroying comments and formatting; one add against a curated, commented config deletes every comment (a review-hostile diff, against the team-workflow story). Append rendered YAML text for the new blocks, or use a round-trip-preserving loader (ruamel.yaml).

P2 -- target_files (configurator.py) duplicates config.py's _resolve_includes with divergent semantics: config errors on an unmatched glob, the configurator silently returns an empty list which Configure's [0] then turns into an IndexError. Unify on one exported resolver.

P2 -- Proposal defaults (max 0.05, vs_previous 0.5/2.0, max_lag 24h) live as literals in cli.py's _build_offered_check, not in configurator.py, despite the module docstring's claim that all proposal logic lives there; the TUI Configure screen therefore has no per-column offers at all. Move _build_offered_check into configurator.py using its constants.

P3 -- Wizard hygiene: the freshness offer hardcodes 24h without prompting; the TUI Configure screen lacks the per-check trim the CLI has.
close_reason: Hardened the add wizard and configurator: secrets-safe entry (interpolate ${VAR} before probing, write the placeholder to YAML), adapter cleanup on all paths, validated numeric/index prompts, check_id dedup across the composed config, is_view into propose_checks (both front-ends), ambiguous-timestamp prompt (CLI + Configure), comment-preserving config writes (text splice with round-trip fallback), unified include resolution (resolve_includes made public), build_offered_check moved into the configurator, and the freshness offer no longer hardcodes 24h. 595 tests. Deferred to df-p16: the TUI per-column offered-checks selection and per-check trim.

title: Expand the README and link the deployed docs site
description: Make README.md more complete (what dbfresh is and does, install, a minimal config plus first run, exit codes, where the CLI/TUI/configurator fit) and link to the deployed GitHub Pages mkdocs site at https://dchud.github.io/dbfresh/ for the full documentation. Concise and accurate to the shipped tool.
close_reason: README expanded: what dbfresh is and does, install (PyPI-pending plus from-source), a minimal sqlite config with a first run and the exit-code table, the CLI/TUI/configurator surfaces, and a prominent link to the docs site. Corrected the .env note to reflect that every config-reading command loads .env (not just run).

title: Observation store: SQLite schema, stable check_id, per-run persistence, history command
description:
Implement store.py with the run/observation SQLite schema; stable check_id derivation (explicit id, else identity-tuple hash with the expectation excluded); persist one observation per check per run including OK and ERROR; --store / --no-store flags; and the dbfresh history command.
Accept: a run writes one observation per check; editing a threshold does not change check_id; history prints recent values. Spec: dbfresh.md section 7.

title: SQL Server and Databricks live connection adapters
description: pymssql and databricks-sql-connector adapter classes over the SQLAlchemy base plus factory registration, with env-gated live integration tests. Dialects and reflection already landed in E1.
close_reason: SQL Server (mssql+pymssql) and native Databricks adapters added with factory registration and contract tests (510 passing; live integration env-gated). Engine freshness_source dispatch is a separate follow-up: df-lsp.

title: Interactive Textual TUI: dashboard, menu, configure screen, history drill-down
description: dbfresh ui Textual app: status dashboard, menu, Configure screen reusing the configurator, history drill-down, in-app run trigger. See dbfresh.md epic E8.
close_reason: Interactive Textual TUI (dbfresh ui): status dashboard from config+store, Configure/Report/History screens reusing existing modules, in-app run via shared runner.py, Pilot-tested. Verified: no CLI regression, all 5 commands present.

title: TUI Configure: pick an existing source and edit an already-written check
description:
GitHub issue #35. ConfigureScreen._propose() (tui/configure.py) always assumes a brand-new source+object: two blank Input fields, no listing of self._config.sources to pick from, and Accept only ever appends new checks via configurator.append_checks -- there's no path back to an already-written check to change its threshold. Partial overlap with df-cpj (adds a value input to *proposed*, not-yet-written checks). Settled design -- full v1 scope, all three tiers:

1. Source picker: replace the blank source-name Input with a picker over self._config.sources.
2. Read-only existing-checks list: show the object's already-written checks (metric, column/key, expectation) below the picker before proposing more -- answers the issue's 'doesn't show the current sources/tables' complaint directly.
3. Inline edit: each existing check gets an editable threshold + Save action. Needs a new configurator primitive that finds a check block by check_id and rewrites just its expect: value.

This is exactly the operation check_id (checks.py) was already designed to support safely: it deliberately hashes only (source, object, metric, column/key) -- never expect -- specifically so tuning a threshold doesn't fork history. Editing in place is a safe, intended operation, just not yet exposed in the TUI.

Rewrite approach: follow the precedent already set by _append_into_block/_write_new_checks -- attempt a text splice that finds the matched check's expect: sub-block and rewrites only that, preserving comments/formatting elsewhere; fall back to a full YAML round-trip only for a shape that can't be safely spliced (same 'return None, caller falls back' pattern already used for appends).

Scope boundary: check thresholds only for v1, not source connection params -- those can carry ${VAR} secrets. Settled: if/when source-param editing is ever built (own future bead, not this one), never display a resolved secret value in the UI -- for any param name _looks_like_secret_key() (cli.py) would flag (token/password/secret/key-ish), show only its ${VAR} name as a reminder that it's edited via .env, mirroring the same hint the add wizard already gives at creation time.
close_reason: Resolved in this PR: TUI Configure now picks an existing source and can edit an already-written check's threshold in place.

title: Repository scaffolding, CI, and packaging
description: README (present) and MIT LICENSE (present); GitHub Actions running just check on push/PR; CONTRIBUTING; PR/issue templates; pre-commit running ruff; packaging + release workflow; optional CODEOWNERS/Dependabot. See dbfresh.md epic E11.
close_reason: CI, CONTRIBUTING, pre-commit, PR template, and packaging metadata merged. Release/publish workflow split to a follow-up.

title: check_id ignores the expectation, so two checks on one source/object/metric/column collide
description: Found while adding df-xwo. check_id is a hash of (source, object, metric, column) and ignores the expectation, so two checks on the same source/object/metric/column but with different expectations share one identity and append_checks silently drops the second. It surfaces when the configurator offers a per-column check (e.g. freshness) on a column propose_checks already auto-proposed the same metric for -- selecting both keeps only one. Decide whether check_id should include a discriminator (a hash of the expectation, or an explicit label) so distinct checks on the same column/metric can coexist, or whether this is acceptable and should just be documented. Not a regression; pre-existing dedup design. Candidate for the df-je2 sign-off to weigh.
close_reason: Configurator no longer offers a per-column check for a metric already auto-proposed for that column (freshness on the auto-detected timestamp column; duplicate_count on a key column that is also numeric/string). offered_column_checks gained a 'proposed' parameter and skips already-covered (metric, column) pairs; both the CLI wizard and TUI Configure pass the proposed bundle. check_id and the history model are untouched. Documented in authoring-checks.md; TUI robustness to hand-tuned values covered by a new test.

title: History-based expectations: vs_previous baseline selection, ratio/delta guards, on_missing
description:
Implement vs_previous with baseline previous | last_same_weekday; ratio and delta guards; on_missing handling.
Accept: a 3x row-count swing vs the correct baseline fails; first run passes under default on_missing. Spec: dbfresh.md section 7.3.
close_reason: Superseded by the E1-E11 epic plan in dbfresh.md

title: TUI visual design pass (Catppuccin Macchiato)
description:
Umbrella for the TUI's first real styling. There is currently no styling at all -- no .tcss file, no CSS_PATH, no DEFAULT_CSS anywhere under src/dbfresh/tui/ -- so every screen is raw default Textual layout: the grid hugs the top-left with jammed single-glyph columns (df-yh1), the Configure form burns half the terminal and shows section headers over empty bodies before any Propose, buttons have no primary/variant styling, and Report/History are monochrome Static dumps.

Adopt the Catppuccin Macchiato palette as the theme. Batch the styling into one coherent pass rather than piecemeal tweaks, since every change churns the four committed SVG snapshot baselines; re-baseline once at the end.

Children: an app-level .tcss + Macchiato palette scaffold (blocks the rest), grid padding/zebra/column widths (df-yh1), Configure layout, and a single snapshot re-baseline.

title: Introduce an app-level stylesheet and adopt the Catppuccin Macchiato palette
description:
There is no styling anywhere under src/dbfresh/tui/ (no .tcss, no CSS_PATH, no DEFAULT_CSS). Add an app-level .tcss and adopt the Catppuccin Macchiato palette as the theme -- define the palette (base, surfaces, text, and the accent set) as CSS variables and wire it via CSS_PATH on DbfreshApp.

This is the scaffold the rest of the visual pass builds on. Map the status colors (OK/WARN/FAIL/ERROR/SKIPPED) onto Macchiato's green/yellow/red/peach/overlay so they stay consistent with the legibility epic.

title: Lay out the Configure screen
description:
The Configure form (configure.py:108-133) stacks raw default widgets: the fixed form consumes about half a 30-row terminal, the three section headers show over empty bodies before any Propose, buttons have no primary/variant styling, and the Select starts blank even with one source.

With the .tcss scaffold in place: group the sections into panels, hide the proposed/offered/existing sections until a Propose populates them, give Propose a primary button variant, style headings, and preselect a lone source.

title: Re-baseline TUI snapshots after the visual pass
description: The four committed SVG baselines under tests/__snapshots__/test_tui_snapshots/ will all change once styling lands. Re-baseline them once, at the end of the visual pass, rather than churning them per-change.

title: TUI: replace the nested Home tree with a source/check-by-day status grid
description:
GitHub issue #33. The Home dashboard (tui/dashboard.py build_dashboard) renders a 4-level nested Tree -- source > object > column > check, every level expand=True -- which burns vertical space (already 2/3 of a terminal for a 2-table demo config) and shows only the latest status, no history. Settled design:

Row granularity: one row per source.object at the top level (not per check) -- reuses the existing worst_status/_worst_or_unknown rollup dashboard.py already computes, so two tables stays two rows regardless of how many checks each has. Selecting a row drills in to that object's own checks, rendered by the *same* grid component scoped one level down (one renderer, two scopes) -- reuses the existing History-drill-down pattern rather than a bespoke screen.

Columns: [overall, Mon..Sun] -- 'overall' is the existing latest-status rollup (unchanged logic, just repositioned as a leading column instead of the row's only signal), plus a fixed 7-day trend from Store.history(check_id, limit=7) applied per day-bucket instead of per-check-latest only.

Day-bucket rules: a day with no run renders dim/blank, reusing the existing _STATUS_STYLE convention for 'unknown' (None: dim). A day with multiple runs shows the worst status of that day, reusing worst_status/_worst_or_unknown again.

Same [overall, Mon..Sun] shape applies one level down in the drill-in view, so both grids read the same way.

Deferred, tracked separately: df-s7a ('days since last issue' streak column), blocked on this bead.
close_reason: Resolved in PR #38 (merged to main): replaced the nested Home tree with a source/check-by-day status grid, ObjectDetailScreen drill-in, and HistoryScreen reuse.

title: Business calendar: weekend/holiday-aware expectations, business-time freshness, skip_on_holiday
description:
Implement calendar.py using the holidays package; by_weekday / on_holiday expectation selection; calendar: business freshness via business_time_between; skip_on_holiday -> SKIPPED.
Accept: a Monday run selects the Monday expectation; Friday-evening data reads as fresh on Monday under the business calendar; a configured holiday is treated as non-business. Spec: dbfresh.md section 6.
close_reason: Superseded by the E1-E11 epic plan in dbfresh.md

title: Add minimal structured logging over the run/source/check lifecycle
description: From the df-v3b consistency sweep: structlog was a declared runtime dependency that nothing imported, so df-v3b drops it. This tracks the alternative the v1 review raised -- add minimal structured logging (run start/end, per-source connect success/failure, per-check ERROR) so the connect-failure class is debuggable from outside. Keep it small: a structlog or stdlib logging setup plus a handful of lifecycle log points, with opt-in verbosity via a flag or env var. Deferred out of df-v3b to keep that a pure cleanup sweep.
close_reason: Added minimal structured logging with structlog (re-added as a runtime dep) rendered to stderr, off by default with -v/-vv and a DBFRESH_LOG_LEVEL override; log points for run start/end (cli), per-source connect (runner), and per-check error/result (engine); config in a new logsetup.py. stdout stays clean; 13 tests.

title: CLI reports config validation errors cleanly instead of a Python traceback
description: load_config raises ValueError for validation problems (unknown source ref, duplicate check_id across composed files, empty include glob, calendar features without a calendar block, schema/vs_previous operator misuse, etc.), which the CLI lets propagate as an unhandled traceback. Introduce a ConfigError (or catch at the run/history/prune boundary) so the CLI prints a single clear 'config error: ...' message to stderr and exits non-zero, per spec sections 11 and 13.
close_reason: CLI prints 'config error: <msg>' to stderr and exits 2 at each load_config boundary instead of a traceback.

title: TUI/CLI: threshold inputs for auto-proposed checks
description:
From the v1 sign-off (df-je2), triaged as a feature gap, not a defect. propose_checks() always proposes freshness (on the auto-detected timestamp column) with a hardcoded max_lag: 24h default -- the proposed-checks section (_mount_proposed_checkboxes in tui/configure.py) shows a checkbox and a static description, no value input, unlike offered checks (which already have inline threshold Inputs -- see df-p16/df-xwo).

df-7p6 (PR #40) added an alternate path: accept the proposed default, then re-open Configure on the same source+object, where the now-written freshness check appears in the new "existing checks" list with an editable Input + Save. That's a real path, not a full substitute -- it requires retyping the same source and object (nothing is remembered across the round trip), a second live introspection call against the actual source, and re-displays the same proposed bundle a second time (which just dedupe-skips on Accept, but reads as confusing), before the user even finds the right check in a separate section further down. Six-plus steps and a redundant network round trip to change one number on a check just created.

Still worth doing: a value Input directly beside the proposed freshness check's checkbox (mirroring the offered-check pattern already in _mount_offered_checkboxes/_OFFERED_VALUE_DEFAULTS), editable before Accept, no round trip needed. null_rate is never in the *proposed* bundle (only offered, which already has this) -- drop that from scope, this is about freshness's max_lag specifically, the one proposed check with a plain-scalar operand. CLI wizard (dbfresh add) parity remains optional/secondary, as originally scoped, and is fully unaddressed by df-7p6 (verified: no existing-check-editing capability there at all).
close_reason: Resolved in this PR: the proposed freshness check gets an editable max_lag Input beside its checkbox, tunable before Accept -- no round trip through accept-then-edit-as-existing needed. CLI wizard parity remains out of scope, as originally marked optional/secondary.

title: Add a dbfresh env command to list a config's required variables and whether they are set
description: Split from df-l1d (the onboarding bead). A 'dbfresh env -c config.yaml' command that scans the raw config YAML for ${VAR} references before interpolation and lists, per variable: the source/field(s) it feeds and whether it is currently set (after the normal .env load). Turns the one-ConfigError-at-a-time loop into a single overview and answers 'which vars does MY config need' regardless of the user-chosen names. It cannot infer value shape, so it complements the Environment & secrets docs page rather than replacing it. Design to settle when picked up: output format (a table; secrets never printed, set/unset only), exit code (informational exit 0, or a preflight gate that exits non-zero when any referenced var is unset), and whether to scan included config files too.

title: TUI status legibility
description:
Umbrella for presentation-correctness fixes on the status grid, Report, and History screens -- all independent of the palette/theming work in the visual-design epic.

The grid cannot distinguish the two most important triage cases: _STATUS_GLYPH and _STATUS_STYLE map both FAIL and ERROR to the same red mark (dashboard.py:29-48), and a code comment there falsely claims color already separates them; SKIPPED and never-observed are both styled dim and read almost identically. The Report screen is monochrome and prefixes WARN/FAIL/ERROR with the same glyph (report.py:155). History prints raw unformatted values (e.g. row_count as 80.0 where the digest shows 80) and leads with the internal check_id hash.

Children make status severity carry visual signal everywhere it is shown.

title: Distinguish ERROR from FAIL in the status grid
description:
_STATUS_GLYPH and _STATUS_STYLE map both FAIL and ERROR to the same red mark (dashboard.py:29-48), so the grid cannot answer the first triage question -- bad data vs unreachable source. The comment at dashboard.py:39-40 falsely claims color already distinguishes them.

Give ERROR a distinct glyph and/or color and fix the comment. Trivial change, high value, independent of any theming.

title: Distinguish SKIPPED from never-observed in the grid
description: SKIPPED and never-observed are both styled dim (dashboard.py:29-48) and read almost identically on screen. Give them distinct enough marks/styles that "deliberately skipped" and "no observation yet" are tellable apart.

title: Add a status glyph legend and label the first grid column
description: The status glyphs have no on-screen legend, and the first grid column header is the empty string (dashboard.py:224). Add a legend (footer line or help overlay) and a meaningful label for the object/check column so the grid is readable without prior knowledge.

title: Color the Report screen by status severity
description:
ReportScreen is a monochrome Static dump of render_digest (screens.py:40-47), and the digest prefixes WARN, FAIL, and ERROR with the same glyph (report.py:155), so a freshness WARN is visually identical to a null_rate FAIL.

Carry status severity into the TUI rendering with per-status color and glyphs -- either a TUI-side coloring of the digest text or a structured TUI renderer.

title: Format History values and drop the check_id hash line
description:
HistoryScreen prints raw unformatted values via render_history (e.g. row_count as 80.0 where the digest shows 80; null_rate unformatted where the digest shows a percentage), and leads with the internal check_id hash line.

Apply the digest's per-metric formatting (report._format_observed, report.py:103-111) to History values and demote or drop the check_id line.

title: load_dotenv runs only for 'run'; history/prune/add/ui skip it
description: Only _run_command calls load_dotenv(config_dir/.env). The history, prune, add, and ui commands load the config (which interpolates ${VAR}) without loading .env first, so a config using ${VAR} fails with 'undefined environment variable' unless those vars are already exported. Load .env once at the CLI boundary for every command that reads config.
close_reason: load_dotenv moved to the CLI dispatch boundary; run/history/prune/add/ui all load .env beside the config.

title: TUI editing lifecycle beyond append
description:
Umbrella for maintaining a config, not just growing it. Today the Configure screen only appends new checks; the one existing-check edit it supports (a single-scalar threshold) is buried inside the add-new flow and gated on a successful live DB connection (configure.py:341-358), so you cannot loosen a freshness threshold while the source is down.

Several capabilities are missing from both the TUI and the CLI/configurator and need backend work first: there is no remove_check, no source edit/remove (only add_source), and rewrite_check_expectation handles only a single nested scalar (configurator.py:813), so between (the most common expectation in the example config) is not editable.

Children, backend-before-UI: surface threshold editing from ObjectDetail decoupled from introspection; an object picker/autocomplete from known config objects; editing between (lo/hi); deleting a check with confirmation; and (larger, deferred) editing/removing a source including secret/${VAR} handling.

title: Surface threshold editing from ObjectDetail, decoupled from live introspection
description:
Existing-check threshold editing lives inside Configure._propose and is only reachable after a successful DB connection (configure.py:341-358), so you cannot adjust a threshold while the source is down. The edit itself (rewrite_check_expectation) needs no connection.

Move single-scalar threshold editing to ObjectDetailScreen, which already scopes to an object without connecting. Highest-leverage item in this epic.

title: Add an object picker/autocomplete in Configure
description:
The object name is free-typed (configure.py:117, 334) with no assistance, even though config.checks already knows every configured object; retyping fully-qualified names exactly is error-prone.

Offer autocomplete or a picker from known config objects (helps both adding checks and editing existing ones).

title: Edit between (lo/hi) expectations
description:
rewrite_check_expectation handles only a single nested scalar (configurator.py:813), so between -- the most common expectation in config.example.yaml and the snapshot fixture -- is read-only in the TUI (configure.py:53-58).

Extend the rewrite backend to a two-scalar operand and add a lo/hi editing UI. Backend before UI.

title: Delete a check
description:
There is no way to remove a check in either the TUI or the CLI -- configurator.py has no remove function; the only path is hand-editing YAML.

Add a remove_check backend that deletes a check by check_id from its source file, then a TUI affordance (from ObjectDetail) with a confirmation step. Backend before UI.

title: Edit or remove a source
description: Only add_source exists (configurator.py:535); there is no edit or remove for a configured source in the TUI or CLI. This needs backend design first, including how connection params and secret/${VAR} references are handled on edit. Larger; deferred behind the check-editing items above.

title: Use SQLite STRICT tables for the observation store
description: Turn on SQLite STRICT typing for the observation store's tables (run, observation) in store.py. Every column already uses a STRICT-legal type (INTEGER/TEXT/REAL), so this is appending STRICT to both CREATE TABLE statements plus a test. STRICT enforces the value/value_text split at the storage layer: a numeric scalar lands in value (REAL), everything else in value_text (TEXT), and a mistyped write is rejected at insert time instead of being silently coerced under type affinity -- the same contract the Python types (split_value, Store.start_run) assert, enforced one layer down. Only affects our store, not the SQLite adapter that reads user tables. Note: CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ... STRICT only applies to newly created databases; an existing dbfresh.db stays non-STRICT until recreated -- acceptable, no real stores exist yet, no migration needed. Requires SQLite >= 3.37, which Python 3.14 bundles comfortably.
close_reason: Enabled SQLite STRICT typing on the run and observation store tables (every column already used a STRICT-legal type). A mistyped write is now rejected at insert (sqlite3.IntegrityError) instead of being coerced under type affinity; test added.

title: Persist richer observations for cross-session TUI detail
description:
Umbrella for the store-schema work that unblocks meaningful cross-session reporting in the TUI. Observation rows persist only value/value_text/status/label (store.py:176-216) -- no expected/threshold, no error text, no samples or diff -- so ReportScreen can only show an in-session digest (screens.py:20-31), History for a past FAIL shows a value with no threshold and for an ERROR shows no message, and there is no latest_run() accessor even though the run table holds the data (store.py:147-175).

The anchor child is the schema change (persist expected + error, optionally samples/diff, add latest_run(), with a migration); the last-run line, cross-session Report, and enriched History are all blocked on it. A TUI prune/retention surface (CLI-only today) rides here too but is independent of the schema change.

title: Persist expected and error per observation and add a latest_run accessor
description:
Observation rows persist only value/value_text/status/label (store.py:176-216) -- no expected/threshold, no error text, no samples or diff -- so no cross-session digest or failure detail can be reconstructed, and there is no latest_run() accessor though the run table holds the data (store.py:147-175).

Extend the observation schema to persist expected and error (optionally samples/diff), add latest_run(), and provide a migration for existing stores. Anchor for this epic; the screens below are blocked on it.

title: Show a last-run context line on Home
description: Home shows no run context. Once latest_run() exists, add a line (config path / store path / last run time and outcome) so the dashboard states when it was last refreshed and against what.

title: Reconstruct the Report screen from the store across sessions
description: ReportScreen only renders the in-session last_run and shows "no run recorded in this session yet" otherwise (screens.py:20-31), because the store lacks the detail. Once observations persist expected/error, render a cross-session report from the store so the digest survives restarting the TUI.

title: Show threshold and error in History rows
description: History for a past FAIL shows a value with no threshold and for an ERROR shows no message, because the store keeps neither. Once persisted, show expected-vs-observed and error text in History rows, and add a depth/limit control (currently fixed at 30, screens.py:80, where the CLI has -n).

title: Add a TUI prune/retention surface with store visibility
description: Prune/retention is CLI-only (cli.py:114-118, 255-276) and the TUI shows nothing about store size or the retention setting. Add a TUI surface to view store size and retention and to run a prune, with a confirmation step. Independent of the schema change.

title: Configurator and add wizard: metadata-driven proposals, connection test, existence checks
description: Front-end-agnostic configurator: metadata-driven proposal bundle, timestamp/key heuristics, category->offer mapping, connection test for new source, object-existence checks, manual fallback; dbfresh add. See dbfresh.md epic E7.
close_reason: Configurator module (metadata-driven proposal bundle, category offers, timestamp/key heuristics, connection test, existence check, include-aware YAML emission) plus the dbfresh add wizard. Verified end-to-end. Closure rides in the Gate 3 PR.

title: Type the seams: adapter/store Protocols and a type checker in CI
description:
From the v1 review (df-x0e). The adapter/store protocol exists only in prose; the annotations that exist are decoration.

P1 -- No typing.Protocol anywhere; the engine types adapter: Any and store: Any throughout (15 Any in engine.py alone); describe_history_last_modified is duck-called on whatever adapter arrives (a non-Databricks adapter would raise AttributeError, silently mapped to ERROR by the broad except). The structural cause: Result/Status live in engine.py and store.py imports them, so the engine cannot name Store without an import cycle -- Any is papering over a layering knot. Move Result/Status (and worst_status/exit_code) to a leaf models module; define Adapter and ObservationReader Protocols in adapters/base.py; type the engine, runner, and configurator against them; add mypy or pyright to just check and CI (with zero enforcement the annotations rot). Specific inaccuracies a checker catches today: Expectation.evaluate(value: float | None) also receives str (schema equals path); Store.start_run -> int but lastrowid is int | None; Result.samples: list is unparameterized; introspection_capabilities is declared on three Dialect subclasses but not the base class (AttributeError on Dialect(), and it is dead code).
close_reason: Typed the adapter/store seams: a leaf models module (breaks the engine<->store import cycle), Adapter/ObservationReader/HistoryAwareAdapter Protocols, mypy added to just check and CI, plus the Expectation.evaluate / Store.start_run / Result.samples type-inaccuracy fixes.

title: TUI navigation hygiene and scale affordances
description:
Umbrella for navigation correctness and the affordances a larger config will need. App-level bindings (r/c/p/q, app.py:35-40) are not suppressed on pushed screens, so on Report/History/Configure the footer still advertises them and pressing p/c pushes another copy of the same screen on top of itself with no guard -- unbounded stacking (inferred from binding mechanics + the footer snapshots; verify live first).

Enter-to-drill into ObjectDetail/History has no footer hint or on-screen cue; escape is labeled Cancel on Configure and Back elsewhere; there is no ? help or glyph legend; and config edited in another window is never reloaded (loads once at mount, app.py:71-78). At scale the Home grid is cursor-only with no filter/search/worst-first sort, and runs are always all-checks with no --only/object-scoped parity (filter_checks exists, cli.py:92).

Children cover the stacking guard + per-screen footers, discoverability/labeling/help + a manual reload binding, and the two lower-urgency scale items.

title: Guard duplicate screen pushes and scope bindings per screen
description:
App-level bindings (r/c/p/q, app.py:35-40) are not suppressed on pushed screens, so Report/History/Configure footers still advertise them and pressing p/c pushes another copy of the same screen on top of itself with no guard -- unbounded stacking (inferred from binding mechanics + footer snapshots; verify live first).

Guard against pushing a screen type already on the stack and give each pushed screen a footer that only advertises keys it handles.

title: Improve navigation discoverability and add help
description:
Enter-to-drill into ObjectDetail/History has no footer hint or on-screen cue; escape is labeled Cancel on Configure (configure.py:90) and Back elsewhere; there is no ? help or glyph legend; and config edited in another window is never reloaded (loads once at mount, app.py:71-78).

Add Enter-to-drill hints, consistent Back/Cancel labeling, a ? help overlay (folding in the glyph legend from the legibility epic), and a manual config-reload binding.

title: Add grid filter, search, and worst-first sort
description: The Home grid is cursor-navigation only (dashboard.py object_rows sorts by source/object); at dozens-to-hundreds of objects there is no way to filter to non-OK rows, search by substring, or sort worst-first. Add these as the object count grows. Lowest urgency until real configs get large.

title: Support scoped runs from the TUI
description: The TUI always runs every check; the CLI has run --only SOURCE via filter_checks (cli.py:92). Add scoped-run parity -- at least an object-scoped "run this object" from ObjectDetail using filter_checks, and optionally a source filter on Home.

title: v1 sign-off: delta review of the post-review follow-ups
description:
v1 sign-off gate: a focused, delta-oriented review of everything done since the original v1 review (df-x0e), for final sign-off. Run it as a fable pass (confirm cost at gate time) AFTER the remaining review follow-ups are merged, against the git diff from the pre-review baseline rather than re-reviewing unchanged code the first review already covered.

Scope:
1. Confirm each of df-x0e's nine findings is genuinely resolved by its follow-up -- not just that a PR merged: df-l9r (fail-clean error boundary and ERROR mapping), df-34m (add wizard / configurator hardening), df-tss (Databricks describe() views), df-rjk (engine/store mechanics), df-ou5 (config schema completeness and validation), df-027 (TUI robustness), df-m3o (reporting contract), df-idc (type-the-seams), df-v3b (consistency sweep).
2. Review the net-new code the fixes introduced, which the first review never saw: assert_sql, source timezone/timeout handling, the models module and the Adapter/ObservationReader Protocols, the type-checker setup and its scoping, the JSON envelope, and the Databricks describe()/is_view path.
3. A lighter integration/regression sweep for interactions arising from the volume of change (the whole error-handling model, config validation, JSON contract, adapters, and the types refactor all moved).
4. Triage the minor deferrals and concerns logged while resolving the findings: df-p16 (deferred TUI per-column offers and per-check trim), the run-row-opened-before-evaluation RUNNING-row edge from df-rjk, assert_sql's capped violation count, a source timezone: string not validated at load time, and the type-checker's scoping choices from df-idc.
5. Review the whole test suite for shape and health, not just correctness of the changes. The suite is large for a tool this young (~695 tests) -- assess whether it is the right shape (the right behaviors tested at the right level: unit vs integration vs TUI/Pilot vs snapshot), whether there are big coverage gaps, and whether it is redundant (duplicated assertions, tests pinned to implementation detail rather than behavior, near-identical cases that should collapse into parametrization). Recommend consolidation where the test count is disproportionate to the surface, and flag genuinely under-tested areas. Propose concrete follow-up beads for both trimming and filling gaps.

File a prioritized follow-up bead for anything unresolved. This is a delta / sign-off pass, deliberately not a full re-review. Blocked until df-idc, df-v3b, df-l1d, and df-p16 are merged.
close_reason: v1 sign-off complete. The nine original review findings all verified genuinely resolved. The fable delta review surfaced two important silent-false-pass bugs (schema-unchanged baseline masking drift after a value-less prior; assert_sql SQL-Server capping corrupting author SQL) plus seven minor issues; all confirmed defects fixed and merged (PR #29 config, #30 TUI, #31 correctness). Test suite assessed as the right shape with modest redundancy trimmed. Deferrals triaged: RUNNING-row edge, type-checker scoping, and the check_id identity model confirmed fine as-is. One feature gap (threshold inputs on auto-proposed checks) tracked as a follow-up.

title: Report all undefined config variables at once, not one at a time
description: interpolate_env in config.py raises on the FIRST undefined ${VAR}, so a config missing three variables costs three fix-run cycles. Make missing-variable reporting accumulate: collect every unset ${VAR} across the main config and all included files, then raise one ConfigError listing them all, before building/validating -- reusing the existing _raise_validation_errors so a single missing var keeps its exact current message and several get the numbered summary. Keep interpolate_env's default (env only) raise-on-first behavior for direct callers.
close_reason: interpolate_env now accumulates undefined ${VAR}s via an optional 'missing' set; _load_config threads one set through the main config and all included files and raises a single ConfigError listing every missing variable before building/validating. Single-missing message unchanged; multiple are comma-joined.

title: Observation store and history: schema, check_id, git_sha, history/prune
description: store.py run/observation schema (incl. value_text), stable check_id, git_sha capture, store-path precedence, dbfresh history and prune. See dbfresh.md epic E3.
close_reason: Observation store, stable check_id, git_sha, persistence, history, and prune implemented and merged via the batch-monitor branch.

title: Environment-variable and secrets onboarding: example files and a reference page
description:
A new user setting up dbfresh must reverse-engineer both WHICH environment variables they need and WHAT shape each value takes, and the config/YAML reference alone does not tell them: the ${VAR} names are user-chosen, missing vars surface one at a time as a fail-fast ConfigError (no batch overview), and the only place showing a concrete connection shape is a single inline comment in docs/configuration.md (SQL Server URL only; Databricks gets no example host/http_path/token). There is no config.example.yaml and no .env.example, despite .gitignore already carving out !.env.example and the spec's architecture layout listing config.example.yaml.

Close the onboarding gap with:

(1) A config.example.yaml + .env.example PAIR at the repo root using canonical variable names (${MSSQL_URL}, ${DATABRICKS_HOST}/${DATABRICKS_HTTP_PATH}/${DATABRICKS_TOKEN}) and literal-shaped example values (e.g. MSSQL_URL=sqlserver://reader:StrongPassw0rd@sqlserver.internal:1433/WarehouseDB, DATABRICKS_HTTP_PATH=/sql/1.0/warehouses/abcdef0123456789), so the two files demonstrate the pattern for both source types together. This finally uses the unused !.env.example gitignore allowance.

(2) A short "Environment & secrets" docs page: a table per source type (field, a canonical example variable name, the value shape, and one line on where to obtain it -- e.g. the Databricks token is a workspace personal access token; the SQL Server URL supports named-instance and ?database= disambiguation and needs its password URL-encoded if it has special characters). Also consolidate the .env behavior here (loaded for run/history/prune/add/ui, real env vars take precedence).

Cross-references: config.example.yaml is currently a P3 sub-item of df-ou5, and the ".env is loaded for all config-reading commands" docs correction is in df-v3b -- this bead subsumes and executes both for the onboarding story, so drop those sub-items there when this lands.

Optional complement (fold in here or split into its own bead when picked up): a dbfresh env -c config.yaml command that scans the raw YAML for ${VAR} references before interpolation and lists each variable, the source/field it feeds, and whether it is currently set (post .env load) -- turning the one-ConfigError-at-a-time loop into a single overview. It answers "which vars does MY config need" regardless of naming; it cannot infer value shape, so it complements the docs page rather than replacing it.

Not part of this bead (already tracked): df-34m covers stopping dbfresh add from writing literal secrets into the tracked config (interpolate ${VAR} before probing, write the uninterpolated form) -- a safety issue distinct from and arguably higher priority than this documentation work.
close_reason: Environment/secrets onboarding: added .env.example (paired with the existing config.example.yaml) and a new Environment & secrets docs page with a per-source-type ${VAR} table (SQL Server url, Databricks host/http_path/token, SQLite database), a consolidated .env behavior section, and cross-links from quickstart and configuration. The optional 'dbfresh env' command was dropped as redundant.

title: Engine core: adapters, dialects, config loading, primitives, builtins, reporting, exit codes
description: Foundation: SQLAlchemy adapter base + reflection describe(), Dialect model, sqlite plus SQL Server and Databricks adapters, config loading (dotenv, env-var interpolation, validation), two primitives, builtin metrics, static and max_lag expectations, threaded engine, rich progress/digest/JSON, exit codes. Full scope and acceptance in dbfresh.md epic E1.
close_reason: Engine core merged in PRs #1-#2: adapters, dialects, all builtin metrics, assertions, --json, per-source threading. Live-warehouse connection adapters split to a follow-up.

title: Fail clean: total error boundary and ERROR mapping
description:
From the v1 review (df-x0e). The operational boundary where a scheduler-driven monitor lives or dies; this epic is the release gate. Includes end-to-end tests of the ERROR paths (currently none reach exit 3).

P0 -- The CLI error boundary catches only ValueError, but load_config and the engine also raise FileNotFoundError, KeyError, and TypeError on ordinary mistakes: run -c missing.yaml gives a FileNotFoundError traceback; a check missing object: raises KeyError (config.py:136); expect: 5 raises TypeError (checks.py:159); a source missing type: raises KeyError. An unhandled traceback exits with code 1, which the documented 0-3 contract defines as WARN -- so a monitoring wrapper treats a crashed run as 'soft-bound violations only'. Introduce a ConfigError raised by load_config for all validation/parse failures (wrap read_text, YAML parse, and field access); catch (ConfigError, OSError, yaml.YAMLError) at each command boundary; add a last-resort handler in main() so nothing exits 1 by accident; give config errors a distinct exit code (currently 2, colliding with FAIL).

P0 -- Unreachable source at connect time crashes the run instead of mapping to ERROR (spec 13). A connection that fails at adapter construction (create_adapter in runner.py's per-source comprehension; SqlAlchemyAdapter.__init__ and dbsql.connect connect eagerly) gives a raw traceback, exit 1, no digest/JSON, no other sources evaluated, nothing persisted, and leaked adapters. Wrap per-source adapter creation; synthesize ERROR results (with the exception text) for every check on a failed source; continue other sources; only build adapters for sources that have checks.

P1 -- A worker-thread exception aborts the whole run. compile_metric_sql is called OUTSIDE the try/except in _evaluate_check (engine.py:123), so a metric typo (never validated at load) raises ValueError inside a ThreadPoolExecutor worker; pool.map re-raises it in the main thread, the run dies, nothing is persisted, and other sources' completed work is discarded. Wrap the whole per-check body so any exception becomes a Status.ERROR Result (upstream, validate metric names -- see the config epic).

P2 -- Adapter close paths are not exception-safe: in run_and_persist's finally, if the first adapter.close() raises, the rest leak; in SqlAlchemyAdapter.close, a failing _conn.close() skips engine.dispose(). Wrap each close in try/except; use try/finally inside close().

P2 -- Error messages are rough: a missing config file gives a raw traceback; the duplicate check_id error is an opaque hash naming neither colliding check (two checks differing only in where: collide by design, spec 8.2, with no hint toward the explicit id: remedy); a mistyped --store silently creates an empty database and reports 'no observations found'. Name both colliding checks (source/object/metric/column/key) plus the id: remedy; warn when history opens a store file it just created.
close_reason: Fail-clean P0 implemented: ConfigError boundary (exit 3, last-resort main() guard), per-source connect failures map to ERROR without aborting the run, an evaluate_check safety net so any escaped exception becomes ERROR (no worker crash), exception-safe adapter closes, and a diagnosable duplicate-check_id message. 561 tests (23 new, each RED-then-GREEN).

title: Wire freshness_source into the engine for describe_history/describe_detail freshness
description: The Check model has no freshness_source field, so a freshness check's freshness_source: describe_history/describe_detail (proposed by the configurator for Databricks tables, emitted by the CLI) is dropped at config load; compile_metric_sql always emits SELECT MAX(column) and _evaluate_freshness always runs it. The Databricks adapter now provides the metadata origins (describe_history_last_modified via DESCRIBE HISTORY filtered to data operations; describe().last_modified via DESCRIBE DETAIL) plus a validate_freshness_source capability/view gate, but nothing calls them. Wire it end-to-end: (1) add a freshness_source field to Check defaulting to 'column' and parse it in config; (2) config validation gates freshness_source on the source's dialect capability and rejects the DESCRIBE forms on views, and makes column optional for the DESCRIBE forms while still requiring it for 'column'; (3) _evaluate_freshness dispatches: 'column' uses MAX(col) SQL as today, 'describe_history' calls adapter.describe_history_last_modified(obj), 'describe_detail' uses adapter.describe(obj).last_modified, then computes lag against the result. Makes the Databricks metadata-freshness path and the adapter capabilities reachable.
close_reason: Wired freshness_source end-to-end: Check field + config parsing, static dialect-capability validation (dialect_for_type + validate_freshness_source), run-time view check, and _evaluate_freshness dispatch to describe_detail/describe_history. 533 tests. Real-Databricks is_view population is a small follow-up: df-y3f.

title: TUI run and propose feedback
description:
Umbrella for making the two database-touching TUI actions -- Run and Propose -- responsive and legible. Today both give near-zero feedback.

Propose (configure.py:135-144, 323-395) runs create_adapter + check_object_exists + describe synchronously in the button handler with no worker, so a slow or unreachable source freezes the whole app for up to the connection timeout with no spinner and no disabled button. Run (app.py:112-120) starts a thread worker but surfaces no in-progress state and no success toast (only the error path notifies, app.py:138-144), and the per-check progress plumbing the CLI uses (report.progress_reporter / on_result, wired at cli.py:196-199) is never passed (app.py:120).

This is the part of the TUI that most reads as unfinished in daily use. Children cover the Propose worker, a run-in-progress indicator plus a success toast, per-check progress, refreshing the topmost pushed screen on run completion, and unifying Configure's split error surfacing.

title: Run Propose in a worker thread with a visible busy state
description:
ConfigureScreen._propose (configure.py:135-144, 323-395) runs create_adapter + check_object_exists + describe synchronously in the button handler with no @work decorator, so Propose against a slow or unreachable source freezes the entire app for up to the source timeout with no spinner, no disabled button, and no feedback.

Move the introspection into a thread worker like the Run action (app.py:116-120): disable Propose and show an in-progress indicator while it runs, then mount the proposal on completion and re-enable. This is the worst responsiveness defect in the TUI.

title: Show run-in-progress state and a success toast with status counts
description:
action_run_checks starts a thread worker (app.py:112-120) but nothing surfaces WorkerState.RUNNING -- no spinner, no status line -- and success is silent (the dashboard refreshes at app.py:135-137 but only the error path notifies, app.py:138-144).

Add a visible in-progress indicator while a run is active and, on success, a toast summarizing counts (e.g. "N ok, M failed, K unreachable"). Also handle the double-press case: a second r silently cancels the first exclusive worker with no notice.

title: Wire per-check progress into the TUI run
description:
run_and_persist accepts the same on_result / progress callback the CLI uses (report.progress_reporter, wired at cli.py:196-199), but the TUI calls it with no callback (app.py:120).

Feed per-check progress into the run-in-progress indicator so a long run shows which checks have completed rather than a single opaque spinner. Depends on the in-progress indicator existing.

title: Refresh the topmost pushed screen when a run completes
description:
on_worker_state_changed refreshes the Home grid (app.py:135-137), but ObjectDetail populates once in on_mount (screens.py:132-139) and Report renders once in compose, so pressing r while viewing a failing object or the report does nothing visible until esc + re-enter.

Refresh whichever screen is on top when a run finishes so the natural "run and watch it recover" gesture works.

title: Unify Configure error surfacing
description: The Configure screen surfaces errors two ways: Save uses toasts (configure.py:304, 311) while Propose writes unstyled inline text into #proposal-text (configure.py:331, 338, 344, 351). Pick one channel for user-facing errors on this screen and apply it consistently.

title: Reporting contract: spec-shape JSON, --only, and progress
description:
From the v1 review (df-x0e).

P1 -- --json omits most of the spec's stable-contract envelope (spec 10.1). Implemented as {status, results}; spec is {status, run_id, started_at, finished_at, counts, results}, each result carrying check_id, source, object, metric, label, tier, status, value, value_text, expected, observed, error, samples. Today results lack tier, value_text, observed, and diff; the schema fingerprint rides in value instead of value_text (inconsistent with the store's own split); run_id and timestamps exist in the store but are never threaded to the renderer. Shipping a wrong 'stable contract' guarantees a breaking change later. Thread run metadata (run_id or null under --no-store, started/finished timestamps) and counts into render_json; add tier (from column/key presence), split value/value_text per the store's rule, include observed and diff; fix the Quickstart JSON example (it also omits the label field the code emits).

P2 -- run has no --only SOURCE and no progress bar / --no-progress, both in the spec (sections 9 and 10.1). rich is declared specifically for progress yet is only used in the TUI, and the Quickstart says --json 'suppresses the progress bar' that does not exist. --only also matters operationally (exclude a down source). Filter checks and adapter construction by --only; add a rich progress bar suppressed under --json/--no-progress/non-TTY.
close_reason: Reporting contract: --json now emits the stable envelope {status, run_id, started_at, finished_at, counts, results} with each result carrying tier, split value/value_text, observed, and diff (split_value moved to engine.py, shared by store and report); RunResult carries run_id/started_at/finished_at from run_and_persist; --only SOURCE filters checks before adapters are built (excluded adapters never constructed) with a clean unknown-source error; a rich progress bar shows M-of-N, suppressed under --json/--no-progress/non-TTY; the quickstart JSON example matches the emitted shape. 690 tests.

title: Baseline scaffold: connection parser, source adapters, two primitives, builtins, YAML config, reporting, engine tests
description:
Package skeleton; connection URL parser (usql/go-mssqldb -> SqlServerParams); SQL Server (pymssql) and Databricks (databricks-sql-connector) adapters behind the scalar/rows/close protocol; the two primitives (metric+expectation, assertion); builtins row_count, null_rate, duplicate_count, sum/avg/min/max, freshness; YAML config with ${VAR} env interpolation; rich progress + plain-text digest + JSON output; exit codes; sqlite-backed engine tests.
Accept: pytest green; digest renders as in dbfresh.md section 9. Spec: dbfresh.md section 13.
close_reason: Superseded by the E1-E11 epic plan in dbfresh.md

title: Dashboard rolls a SKIPPED-only node up to OK instead of SKIPPED
description: worst_status maps rank 0 back to OK, and SKIPPED shares rank 0 with OK, so worst_status({SKIPPED}) returns OK. The TUI dashboard reuses worst_status for node rollups (_worst_or_unknown in tui/dashboard.py), so a source/object/column whose only known child is SKIPPED displays [OK] even though the leaf itself shows [SKIPPED] — a green all-clear for a check that was intentionally not run. Exit-code aggregation correctly treats SKIPPED as 0, so the fix belongs in the dashboard rollup, not in worst_status: when all known children are SKIPPED, roll the node up to SKIPPED. This will change the TUI snapshot baseline for the affected column.
close_reason: Dashboard rollup returns SKIPPED when all known children are SKIPPED; worst_status unchanged; one baseline regenerated.

title: Business calendar: weekday/holiday overrides, business-time freshness, skip_off_schedule
description: calendar.py with holidays package; by_weekday/on_holiday selection; calendar: business freshness; skip_off_schedule to SKIPPED. See dbfresh.md epic E4.
close_reason: Business calendar (holidays), by_weekday/on_holiday overrides, business-time freshness, and skip_off_schedule implemented.

title: Remove spelled-out 'spec section N' references from code and tests
description: The earlier cleanup removed $-sigil section references but missed spelled-out ones. Six remain: src/dbfresh/calendar.py (module docstring 'spec section 7', class 'spec section 7.1', method 'spec section 7.3'), src/dbfresh/store.py (module 'spec section 8'), src/dbfresh/config.py (StoreConfig 'spec section 8.1'), tests/test_configurator_offers.py (module 'spec section 11.2'). Replace each with a plain description of what the code does, no section pointer. These are process artifacts that should not live in code/tests per the project convention.
close_reason: Removed the six spelled-out 'spec section N' references the earlier sigil-based cleanup missed (calendar.py module/class/method, store.py module, config.py StoreConfig, test_configurator_offers.py module); each docstring now describes the code plainly.

title: Config schema completeness and validation
description:
From the v1 review (df-x0e). Implement or excise every documented-but-missing config field, and bring validation up to spec 13 (runs before any connection, reports all problems at once). Validating metric names here also removes the worker-crash in the fail-clean epic.

P1 -- Source timezone: and timeout: are specified and documented but unimplemented. Any extra source param raises TypeError at adapter construction, so the spec's and docs/configuration.md's own example config cannot run. And engine._to_aware_utc unconditionally assumes naive timestamps are UTC, so freshness lag for a non-UTC source (SQL Server datetime columns are typically naive) is silently off by the offset -- the exact class of silent wrongness the tool exists to catch. Accept timezone:/timeout:, thread the source timezone into freshness evaluation, pass timeout to the drivers, reject genuinely unknown source params with a clean validation error.

P1 -- assert_sql: is documented in three docs pages and referenced by spec 8.2/15 but does not exist; a check with assert_sql parses as metric-less and crashes with 'unsupported metric: None'. Implement raw assertion queries (compile to the dialect-capped fetch, persist the violation count, include the normalized text in check_id) or excise it from docs and make a no-metric-no-assert check a validation error.

P1 -- Validation is far short of spec: metric typos (rowcount) pass then crash mid-run; null_rate without column compiles the literal 'None' into SQL; duplicate_count without key likewise; unknown check-block keys are ignored (typos like colum:); an invalid severity behaves as error; a metric check with no expect: is always-OK; by_weekday/on_holiday on assertions are ignored; max_lag on non-freshness metrics is ignored; errors raise on the first problem instead of accumulating. Validate metric names against registry.METRICS (it exists and is parity-tested), require the discriminating field per metric, require an expectation on metric checks, whitelist check-block keys, validate severity, restrict max_lag to freshness, and report all errors at once.

P1 -- skip_on_holiday alias (spec 7.4, docs/calendar.md) is silently ignored; a documented safety field is a no-op, so the check still runs on holidays. Accept the alias or remove it from spec and docs.

P3 -- config.example.yaml is in the spec's architecture layout but does not exist; add a copyable example file.
close_reason: Config schema completeness and validation. Up-front validation now accumulates and reports every problem at once (registry-checked metric names, required discriminating field, required expectation, whitelisted check keys, valid severity, max_lag only on freshness, plus the existing source-ref / freshness-capability / duplicate-id / calendar checks). Sources take first-class timezone/timeout and reject genuinely-unknown params via adapter-signature introspection. Naive freshness timestamps are interpreted in the source timezone. skip_on_holiday alias honored. assert_sql implemented. config.example.yaml added. 658 tests.

title: TUI Configure: per-column offered checks and per-check trim
description: Deferred from df-34m. The CLI add wizard offers per-column checks (the 'Offered for X: null_rate, sum, ...' step) and lets the user trim proposed checks; the TUI Configure screen has neither, so the two authoring surfaces are not at parity. Add a per-column offered-checks selection and a per-check trim UI to the Configure screen. These are more substantial Textual UI additions (widgets plus wiring) than the rest of df-34m, hence deferred.
close_reason: Added per-column offered-checks selection and per-check trim to the TUI Configure screen, reaching parity with the CLI add wizard on which checks to include and dropping proposed ones. Reuses offered_column_checks/build_offered_check/append_checks; Pilot tests plus a regenerated Configure snapshot. Offered-check threshold customization deferred to df-xwo.

title: Reduce friction in discovering a config's required environment variables
description:
Onboarding: make it easier to learn which environment variables a config needs before running. Two candidate approaches -- pick one or neither.

(a) A 'dbfresh env -c config.yaml' command that scans the raw config YAML for ${VAR} references before interpolation and lists, per variable, the source/field(s) it feeds and whether it is set after the normal .env load. A preflight for 'is my environment ready'. If built: table output with secrets never printed (set/unset only); decide exit code (informational 0 vs a gate that fails when any referenced var is unset); decide whether to scan included config files.

(b) Leaner alternative: interpolate_env in config.py raises on the FIRST undefined ${VAR}, so a config missing three variables costs three fix-run cycles. Make that error accumulate and report ALL missing variables at once, matching the batch config validation already in _validate_sources / _validate_checks. Fixes the real 'one error at a time' friction inside the normal load, no new command surface.

Recommendation: (b). It addresses the actual pain with less surface; (a) mostly re-lists ${VAR}s the author already wrote and cannot infer value shape (the Environment & secrets docs page covers shape). Split from df-l1d; undecided pending maintainer input.

Move it there with a short header noting it is the original spec, and update the CLAUDE.md Key Files reference. title: Engine and store mechanics: dedupe, evaluation-time persistence, WAL
description:
From the v1 review (df-x0e).

P2 -- Engine boilerplate is duplicated five times over: the try/except returning a Status.ERROR Result (in _evaluate_check, _evaluate_assertion, _evaluate_freshness, _evaluate_schema, _evaluate_vs_previous), the WARN/FAIL severity ternary, and twelve near-identical Result(object=..., metric=..., source=...) constructions. engine.py is the largest module at 532 lines and every new metric adds another copy. Extract _error_result(check, exc, ...), _verdict(check, passed), and a _result(check, ...) factory; the module drops about 80 lines and new evaluators become uniform.

P2 -- run_and_persist threads now into evaluation but NOT into start_run/record_observation, which stamp wall-clock time -- evaluation and its recorded observation can disagree (matters for last_same_weekday's 6-day floor) and frozen-clock persistence testing is impossible. Also start_run is called AFTER all checks complete, so started_at is approximately finished_at and both post-date the run, observations get persist-time observed_at, and a mid-run crash persists nothing. Thread now (started_at/observed_at) through persistence; start the run row and capture git_sha before evaluation; stamp results with their evaluation time.

P2 -- record_observation commits per row; a 200-check run is 200 fsyncs. Wrap the persist loop in one transaction.

P2 -- Store durability/concurrency posture is implicit: one sqlite3 connection, check_same_thread=False, no lock, shared across worker threads -- safe today ONLY by an unenforced invariant (workers read-only during evaluation, all writes post-run on one thread) that moving start_run earlier would break. No WAL mode or busy_timeout, so two overlapping dbfresh run processes (cron overlap) hit 'database is locked'. Add PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL and PRAGMA busy_timeout=5000 at open; add a threading.Lock around store methods or an explicit asserted read-only-during-run invariant.
close_reason: Engine Result plumbing deduped (_result/_error_result/_verdict, byte-exact); run row opened before evaluation with started_at=now and observations stamped observed_at=now (evaluation time); observations written in one transaction per run via record_observations; store opens with PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL + busy_timeout for cross-process cron overlap; the single-writer-during-evaluation invariant is documented. 608 tests.

title: Release and publish workflow (PyPI)
description: GitHub Actions release workflow to build and publish the wheel/sdist on tag, enabling the Quickstart's uv tool install / pipx install.

title: TUI: 'days since last issue' streak column on the status grid
description: Follow-on to df-bg6 (the source/check-by-day status grid), deferred for later -- not urgent. Idea from discussing df-bg6: an additional leading column (alongside 'overall' and the day-by-day trend cells) showing how many days have elapsed since that row's (object's or check's) last non-OK observation -- an uptime-style streak counter. Needs df-bg6's grid to exist first. Technical wrinkle: this can't just read off the grid's fixed 7-day trend window -- a healthy check with no recent failure needs to look back further than 7 days to find its last issue, which means its own store query beyond what the trend columns already fetch. Also bounded by retention: Store.prune()/retain_days means observations eventually age out, so a check with no failure in the retained window should read as 'no issue in recorded history' (or similar), not a false 'never failed' claim beyond what's actually knowable.

title: TUI/CLI: format check values by metric type instead of raw floats
description:
GitHub issue #34. render_digest/render_history (report.py) and the TUI's Report/History screens (which reuse those same render functions) print every observed value as a bare Python scalar with no metric-aware formatting. Settled design:

Per-check block restructure: lead each failing check's digest block with the human summary (result.expected, already Expectation.describe() output -- e.g. 'max_lag 24h' or 'vs_previous(previous) ratio [0.5, 2.0]') on its own/header line, instead of sharing a line with 'observed'; give the formatted observed value its own line.

Per-metric formatting rules (display-only, no change to stored precision):
- freshness -> human duration, two significant units ('5d 9h', '45m', '12s')
- freshness only, additionally: append the absolute last-update timestamp in parens, e.g. 'observed: 5d 9h stale (last update: 2026-07-09T03:42:14-04:00)'. Reconstructed at render time as t0 = reference_time - timedelta(seconds=lag_value) -- exact, not approximate, because runner.py resolves now once per run and reuses that same value both for every check's lag computation and as the persisted observed_at for that run's observations. reference_time is run.started_at for the live post-run digest, row['observed_at'] for each history row. No schema change, no new Result field needed. Reuse format_timestamp() (the same ISO-8601 helper already used for the report header and history rows) rather than a second date style.
- duplicate_count -> plain integer
- row_count -> plain integer too (not named in the issue, but same COUNT(*)-is-always-integral situation as duplicate_count; currently prints '60.0')
- null_rate -> percentage, 1 decimal ('4.0%')
- sum / avg / min / max -> rounded to 2 decimals

Also settled: display_timezone() (report.py) currently falls back to None (rendered as UTC/Z) whenever no business calendar is configured -- the common case. Change that fallback to the local system timezone instead of UTC, so the report header, history rows, and the new freshness parenthetical are all local by default and consistent with each other, still ISO 8601 (format_timestamp already renders a numeric +HH:MM/-HH:MM offset for any non-zero-offset tz, not just Z). A configured business calendar's zone still wins, unchanged. No-op on a server already running in UTC.

Also asked for: a one-line human-readable expectation summary per check -- resolved above as leading each block with result.expected.
close_reason: Resolved in PR #36 (merged to main): metric-aware value formatting in report.py, plus display_timezone() defaulting to local system time.

title: Databricks describe() ordering breaks views
description:
From the v1 review (df-x0e).

P1 -- DatabricksAdapter.describe() unconditionally runs DESCRIBE DETAIL (valid only for Delta tables) before computing is_view. On a view (a supported v1 target: Delta tables and views, spec 1) DESCRIBE DETAIL raises, so describe() raises: metric: schema on a Databricks view is ERROR every run; the freshness is_view guard surfaces a raw DESCRIBE error instead of the intended 'views must use a timestamp column' message; and the configurator cannot introspect views at all. Not live-testable here, but the call order is demonstrably wrong. Determine is_view first and skip DESCRIBE DETAIL for views (also wrap it defensively for non-Delta tables); add a contract-level test with a fake cursor that raises on DESCRIBE DETAIL for a view. While there, DESCRIBE HISTORY is fetched unbounded -- add a LIMIT.
close_reason: DatabricksAdapter.describe() computes is_view before last_modified and skips DESCRIBE DETAIL for views (which it is invalid against); _describe_detail_last_modified degrades to None on any failure (non-Delta tables); DESCRIBE HISTORY is bounded with LIMIT 20. 598 tests (+3).

title: Schema (shape) check: fingerprint, equals/unchanged, drift rendering
description: Fingerprint over describe() columns; equals (pinned) and unchanged (vs previous) expectations; drift rendering in the digest. See dbfresh.md epic E2.

title: Consistency sweep: docs accuracy, layering, dead code, nits
description:
From the v1 review (df-x0e). Cross-cutting consistency, dead code, and docs accuracy.

P2 -- Docs stale or self-contradictory in load-bearing places: Quickstart and configuration.md say only run loads .env (false: all five config-reading commands do); configuration.md says the SQL Server/Databricks dialects are ahead of their live adapters (stale: the adapters shipped); history.md says vs_previous can combine with a static bound (contradicts the one-expectation-per-check rule); team-workflow.md says the TUI Configure asks which include file receives a block (it writes to target_files first entry without asking). One docs-accuracy sweep.

P2 -- validate_freshness_source and the describe_history_last_modified adapter method live in adapters/databricks.py but are imported/called by config.py and engine.py -- a generic function and a de-facto fifth adapter-protocol method sitting in an engine-specific module, against the engine-agnostic layering the spec repeats. Move validate_freshness_source (and the DESCRIBE-source set) into adapters/base.py; document describe_history_last_modified as the contract obligation of any dialect declaring describe_history.

P3 -- introspection_capabilities is declared, tested, and documented but read by nothing; the base Dialect and SqliteDialect do not declare it (sqlite exposes keys via reflection, so it should declare keys). Wire the configurator's degradation messaging to it or trim the docs claim and add a base-class default.

P3 -- structlog is a declared runtime dependency but is never imported; there is no logging at all. Drop the dependency or add minimal structured logging (run start/end, per-source connect, per-check ERROR) -- the latter would help debug the connect-failure class.

P3 -- Dead code with a per-run cost: BusinessCalendar.previous_business_day has zero production callers; ObjectInfo.approx_row_count is populated by the Postgres and SQL Server adapters at the cost of an extra catalog query on every describe() (sys.dm_db_partition_stats / pg_class) and is read by nothing -- every SQL Server schema check pays a query per run for an unused value. Wire a consumer or stop fetching.

P3 -- Catalog queries interpolate object names into quoted string literals (databricks table_name equals, mssql OBJECT_ID, pg to_regclass). Consistent with the trusted-config boundary (verified applied everywhere else; store queries are all parameterized), but a name containing a quote breaks the SQL, and the wizard/TUI feed user-typed object names into this path. Use the connector's parameter support or escape.

P3 -- Consistency nits: store.py's unparenthesized multi-except (valid PEP 758 but visually confusing -- parenthesize); adapters/base.py _split_object uses rpartition so db.schema.table on SQL Server mis-splits (document two-part support or split correctly); _ui_command parses the config twice (once to validate, once in the app); docsgen walks argparse privates (brittle, docs-only, add a comment); conftest patches a third-party attribute without a version guard; the copy-pasted seed/config test helpers should move to conftest fixtures.

P3 -- Repo scaffolding: GitHub issue templates (required by the E11 scaffolding scope) are absent -- only a PR template exists.
close_reason: Consistency sweep: corrected stale docs (.env loading, dialects-vs-adapters, vs_previous, TUI Configure); moved validate_freshness_source into adapters/base.py; made introspection_capabilities accurate and wired a configurator consumer; dropped unused structlog (logging deferred to df-c1l); removed dead code (previous_business_day, approx_row_count and its per-run catalog query, dropping the now-inaccurate 'stats' capability); bound object names in Databricks catalog queries; consistency nits; added GitHub issue templates.

Roughly 97 § references across ~35 files plus a few epic labels — (E8) in configurator.py, §16 E10 in test_postgres_adapter.py, §1/§5.4 in adapters/postgres.py. Also fix the § strings inside docsgen.py output, since they render into the generated reference pages. Land as its own PR after the v1 PR merges (needs the E7-E10 files on main).
